papers.title: Hypervelocity Stars from a Supermassive Black Hole-Intermediate-mass Black Hole binary
papers.author_names: Alexander Rasskazov Giacomo Fragione Nathan W. C. Leigh Hiromichi Tagawa Alberto Sesana Adrian Price-Whelan Elena Maria Rossi

abstract.text: In this paper we consider a scenario where the currently observed hypervelocity stars in our Galaxy have been ejected from the Galactic center as a result of dynamical interactions with an intermediate-mass black hole (IMBH) orbiting the central supermassive black hole (SMBH). By performing 3-body scattering experiments, we calculate the distribution of the ejected stars' velocities given various parameters of the IMBH-SMBH binary: IMBH mass, semimajor axis and eccentricity. We also calculate the rates of change of the BH binary orbital elements due to those stellar ejections. One of our new findings is that the ejection rate depends (although mildly) on the rotation of the stellar nucleus (its total angular momentum). We also compare the ejection velocity distribution with that produced by the Hills mechanism (stellar binary disruption) and find that the latter produces faster stars on average. Also, the IMBH mechanism produces an ejection velocity distribution which is flattened towards the BH binary plane while the Hills mechanism produces a spherically symmetric one. The results of this paper will allow us in the future to model the ejection of stars by an evolving BH binary and compare both models with \\\\textit{Gaia} observations, for a wide variety of environments (galactic nuclei, globular clusters, the Large Magellanic Clouds, etc.).

brief.tldr: [theoretical astrophysics research paper] The paper uses 3-body scattering simulations with the ARCHAIN algorithm to model hypervelocity star (HVS) ejection mechanisms in SMBH-IMBH binary systems, finding that IMBH-driven ejections produce velocity distributions flattened toward the binary plane—unlike the spherically symmetric Hills mechanism—which yields faster average velocities. The ejection rate mildly depends on the stellar nucleus' total angular momentum, and the study corrects a prior error in eccentricity evolution rates, enabling future comparison with Gaia data across galactic environments.

source_fields.title: Hypervelocity Stars from a Supermassive Black Hole-Intermediate-mass Black Hole binary

source_fields.sections_text: Method: The paper proposes a scattering simulation method using algorithmic regularization (AR-CHAIN) to model star ejection velocities from SMBH-IMBH binaries, treating stellar orbits as Keplerian when far from the binary to reduce computational cost, and sampling a wide range of initial conditions to generate a statistically robust distribution of high-velocity star (HVS) parameters under physically motivated assumptions. Footnotes: The section discusses the behavior of corotating and counterrotating orbits in binary systems, contrasting it with equal-mass binaries and noting the assumption of full regime refilling of the loss cone in binary orbit dynamics. Conclusions: Our 3-body scattering simulations show that HVS velocities follow a $v^{-4}$ power law, ejection directions are concentrated in the binary plane with a peak near $\\pi$ rather than $3\\pi/2$, and the binary eccentricity evolves negatively at low IMBH masses, suggesting a circular orbit, with these results being robust even when accounting for loss cone depletion and stellar nucleus rotation. Experiments: The experiment finds that the mass ejection rate $J$ and eccentricity evolution rate $K$ depend strongly on binary hardness, initial eccentricity $e$, and stellar rotation, with best-fit parameters showing $J \\approx 0.2$–$0.9$ and $K \\approx 0.7$–$2.5$ for different $q$ and $e$, indicating that binary hardening and ejection are significantly influenced by dynamical and rotational effects in stellar clusters. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel data mining approach to efficiently identify rare hypervelocity stars from Gaia's full 5-dimensional parameter space, addressing the critical problem of detecting unbound HVSs amidst a vast stellar sample by overcoming the limitations of existing methods in distinguishing true HVS candidates from background halo stars. Hills Mechanism: The Hills mechanism describes how a binary star system tidally disrupts when approaching a supermassive black hole within its tidal radius, leading to outcomes such as the ejection of a high-velocity star (HVS) and the formation of a bound S-star, with the ejection occurring primarily for binaries on parabolic orbits due to energy conservation. Mechanisms For Producing Hypervelocity Stars: This section reviews mechanisms for producing hypervelocity stars, primarily through binary disruption by supermassive or intermediate-mass black holes in the Galactic Center and globular clusters, with each mechanism predicting distinct velocity distributions and potential observational signatures.

title: Hypervelocity Stars from a Supermassive Black Hole-Intermediate-mass Black Hole binary

catalog_assessment.evidence: Purely theoretical paper using 3-body scattering simulations to model ejection mechanisms. No observational stellar data presented.

papers.title: Gravitational interactions of stars with supermassive black hole binaries - II. Hypervelocity stars
papers.author_names: Siva Darbha Eric R. Coughlin Daniel Kasen Eliot Quataert

abstract.text: Supermassive black holes (SMBHs) in galactic nuclei can eject hypervelocity stars (HVSs). Using restricted three-body integrations, we study the properties of stars ejected by circular, binary SMBHs as a function of their mass ratios $q = M_2 / M_1$ and separations $a$, focusing on the stellar velocity and angular distributions. We find that the ejection probability is an increasing function of $q$ and $a$, and that the mean ejected velocity scales with $q$ and $a$ similar to previous work but with modified scaling constants. Binary SMBHs tend to eject their fastest stars toward the binary orbital plane. We calculate the ejection rates as the binary SMBHs inspiral, and find that they eject stars with velocities $v_\\\\infty > 1000$ km/s at rates of $\\\\sim 4 \\\\times 10^{-2} - 2 \\\\times 10^{-1}$ yr$^{-1}$ for $q = 1$ ($\\\\sim 10^{-4} - 10^{-3}$ yr$^{-1}$ for $q = 0.01$) over their lifetimes, and can emit a burst of HVSs with $v_\\\\infty > 3000$ km/s as they coalesce. We integrate the stellar distributions over the binary SMBH inspiral and compare them to those produced by the "Hills mechanism" (in which a single SMBH ejects a star after tidally separating a binary star system), and find that $N \\\\sim 100$ HVS velocity samples with $v_\\\\infty \\\\gtrsim 200$ km/s are needed to confidently distinguish between a binary and single SMBH origin.

brief.tldr: [research paper] This study uses restricted three-body integrations to model hypervelocity star (HVS) ejection from circular supermassive black hole binaries, revealing that ejection probability and mean velocity scale with mass ratio $q$ and binary separation $a$, with faster stars preferentially ejected into the orbital plane. Ejection rates reach $\sim 4 \times 10^{-2} - 2 \times 10^{-1} \, \mathrm{yr}^{-1}$ for $q=1$ and peak during binary coalescence with bursts of stars exceeding 3000 km/s. The velocity and angular distributions differ significantly from the Hills mechanism, requiring $\sim 100$ HVS samples with $\nu_{\infty} \gtrsim 200 \, \mathrm{km/s}$ to distinguish between single and binary SMBH origins.

source_fields.title: Gravitational interactions of stars with supermassive black hole binaries - II. Hypervelocity stars

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Conclusions: This study shows that binary supermassive black holes eject hypervelocity stars with velocity and direction dependencies that depend on mass ratio and separation, providing insights into black hole progenitors and galactic environments. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel framework that distinguishes between hypervelocity star production via the Hills mechanism and the SMBH binary (SMBHB) mechanism by leveraging the velocity distribution and ejection rates, offering a new method to probe the presence and properties of SMBH binaries in galactic centers. S. Darbha et al: This section derives and analyzes the velocity and angular distributions of hypervelocity stars ejected from binary supermassive black hole systems, using non-dimensionalized variables to characterize their ejection properties. Simulation Setup: This section describes a simulation setup in which hypervelocity stars ejected from hard binary supermassive black hole systems are modeled using Newtonian dynamics in the full loss cone regime, with key parameters defined in dimensionless units and focusing on the dependence of ejection rates on binary mass ratio and tidal radius. Hypervelocity Stars: The ejection probability of hypervelocity stars from binary supermassive black hole systems increases with both binary separation and mass ratio, and is largely independent of these parameters at large separations, while being lower than the escape probability due to the weak binding of stars on highly elliptical orbits. Comparison With The Hills Mechanism: This section compares the integrated velocity and spatial distributions of hypervelocity stars produced by the Hills mechanism and binary supermassive black hole (SMBH) interactions, showing how their distinct statistical properties can be used to distinguish between these formation mechanisms.

abstract: Supermassive black holes (SMBHs) in galactic nuclei can eject hypervelocity stars (HVSs).

Using restricted three-body integrations, we study the properties of stars ejected by circular, binary SMBHs as a function of their mass ratios $q = M_2 / M_1$ and separations $a$, focusing on the stellar
Binary SMBHs tend to eject their fastest stars toward the binary orbital plane.

We calculate the ejection rates as the binary SMBHs inspiral, and find that they eject stars with velocities $v_\\\\infty > 1000$ km/s at rates of $\\\\sim 4 \\\\times 10^{-2} - 2 \\\\times 10^{-1}$ yr
title: Gravitational interactions of stars with supermassive black hole binaries - II. Hypervelocity stars

papers.title: Gaia and HST astrometry of the very massive $\\\\sim$150 $M_\\\\odot$ candidate runaway star VFTS682
papers.author_names: M. Renzo S. E. de Mink D. J. Lennon I. Platais R. P. van der Marel E. Laplace J. M. Bestenlehner C. J. Evans V. Hénault-Brunet S. Justham A. de Koter N. Langer F. Najarro F. R. N. Schneider J. S. Vink

abstract.text: How very massive stars form is still an open question in astrophysics. VFTS682 is among the most massive stars known, with an inferred initial mass of $\\\\sim$150 $M_\\\\odot$ . It is located in 30 Doradus at a projected distance of 29 pc from the central cluster R136. Its apparent isolation led to two hypotheses: either it formed in relative isolation or it was ejected dynamically from the cluster. We investigate the kinematics of VFTS682 as obtained by Gaia and Hubble Space Telescope astrometry. We derive a projected velocity relative to the cluster of $38 \\\\pm 17 \\\\mathrm{km \\\\ s^{-1}}$ (1$\\\\sigma$ confidence interval). Although the error bars are substantial, two independent measures suggest that VFTS682 is a runaway ejected from the central cluster. This hypothesis is further supported by a variety of circumstantial clues. The central cluster is known to harbor other stars more massive than 150 $M_\\\\odot$ of similar spectral type and recent astrometric studies on VFTS16 and VFTS72 provide direct evidence that the cluster can eject some of its most massive members, in agreement with theoretical predictions. If future data confirm the runaway nature, this would make VFTS682 the most massive runaway star known to date.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The kinematics of the extremely massive star VFTS682 (initial mass ∼150 M⊙) in 30 Doradus are analyzed using *Gaia* DR2 and HST astrometry, yielding a projected velocity relative to the R136 cluster of 38±17 km s⁻¹, consistent with dynamical ejection. This supports the hypothesis that VFTS682 is a runaway star ejected from the central cluster, bolstered by prior evidence of ejections in VFTS16 and VFTS72 and theoretical predictions of cluster-driven ejection of massive stars.

source_fields.title: Gaia and HST astrometry of the very massive $\\sim$150 $M_\\odot$ candidate runaway star VFTS682

source_fields.venue: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society: Letters
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society: Letters
source_fields.sections_text: Discussion: VFTS682 is likely the most massive runaway star known, with a projected velocity of $38\\pm 17\\,\\mathrm{km\\,s^{-1}}$ relative to R136 and ejected $0.7\\pm 0.3$ Myr ago, suggesting dynamical ejection from a massive binary (total mass $\\gtrsim 300\\,M_\\odot$) rather than isolated star formation, and implying the presence of several very massive stars in R136’s core. Affiliations: This section lists the institutional affiliations of researchers involved in an astronomical study. Introduction: This paper makes a novel contribution by providing a comprehensive analysis of the dynamical ejection hypothesis for VFTS682, leveraging multi-wavelength observations and kinematic data to resolve the debate on whether this extremely massive, isolated star formed in situ or was ejected from the R136 cluster, thereby addressing a key problem in understanding the origin of isolated massive stars. Observations: VFTS682 is a massive star with a present-day mass of ~138 M⊙, an age of ~1 Myr, and a kinematic trajectory consistent with being a member of the R136 cluster, though its proper motion suggests a possible ejection or dynamical origin. The kinematics of VFTS682: The kinematic data from Gaia and HST support the hypothesis that VFTS682 is a runaway star dynamically ejected from the R136 cluster, with a kinematic age consistent with an early ejection.

Although the error bars are substantial, two independent measures suggest that VFTS682 is a runaway ejected from the central cluster.

The central cluster is known to harbor other stars more massive than 150 $M_\\\\odot$ of similar spectral type and recent astrometric studies on VFTS16 and VFTS72 provide direct evidence that the cluster

papers.title: A likely runaway star cluster in the outer disc of the Large Magellanic Cloud
papers.author_names: Andrés E. Piatti Ricardo Salinas Eva K. Grebel

abstract.text: We present results from photometric and spectroscopic data obtained with SOAR and Gemini observatory facilities in the field of a recently discovered star cluster. The cluster, projected towards the Eastern side of the outer disc of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC), was originally placed nearly 10 kpc behind the LMC with an age and metallicity typical of the innermost LMC star cluster population. We assigned radial velocity (RV) memberships to stars observed spectroscopically, and derived the cluster age and distance from theoretical isochrone fitting to the cluster colour-magnitude diagram. The new object turned out to be a 0.9 Gyr old outer LMC disc cluster, which possibly reached the present position after being scattered from the innermost LMC regions where it might have been born. We arrived at this conclusion by examining the spatial distribution of LMC star clusters of similar age, by comparing the derived spectroscopic metallicity with that expected for an outside-in galaxy formation scenario, by considering the cluster internal dynamical stage as inferred from its derived structural parameters and by estimating the circular velocity of a disc that rotates with the corresponding star cluster radial velocity at the cluster's deprojected distance, which resulted to be nearly 60 per cent higher than that of most of the outer LMC disc clusters.

brief.tldr: [research paper] A photometric and spectroscopic study of a star cluster in the outer disc of the Large Magellanic Cloud (LMC) reveals it is a 0.9 Gyr-old, metal-rich (Fe/H ∼ -0.1 dex) object located at an unexpected distance, suggesting it was ejected from the inner LMC. Its radial velocity and deprojected distance imply a circular velocity 60% higher than typical outer LMC clusters, supporting a dynamical origin via scattering rather than formation in the outer disc. Structural and kinematic analysis rules out a primordial outer-disc origin, indicating instead a possible runaway history from inner LMC regions during galaxy interactions.

source_fields.title: A likely runaway star cluster in the outer disc of the Large Magellanic Cloud

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Conclusions: The study provides strong evidence that the LMC star cluster was formed in the inner LMC disc and ejected outward, reaching a distance comparable to the SMC, based on its kinematics, distance, age, metallicity, and structural properties. Introduction: This paper presents the first detailed photometric and spectroscopic analysis of a young, metal-rich LMC outer disc cluster located at an unexpected LMC-centric distance, challenging existing models of cluster formation and revealing it as a potential relic of dynamical ejection during the Milky Way–Magellanic Clouds interaction. Observational data: The SOAR/SAMI observations, conducted with adaptive optics and detailed photometric reduction, enabled high-precision $g$ and $i$-band imaging of the star cluster field, yielding accurate photometry and completeness curves for cluster members. Analysis and discussion: The cluster radius was determined to be 31.6 ± 8.0 arcsec (log(r/arcsec) = 1.55 ± 0.10), in excellent agreement with prior estimates, and the background-subtracted radial profile reveals a more detailed and extended structure than previous studies, achieved through a continuous density map using KDE rather than star counts. Radial velocity and metallicity measurements: Radial velocities and metallicities of selected stars were determined using cross-correlation and CaII triplet line equivalent width measurements, leading to a derived cluster radial velocity of 215.3 ± 3.1 km s⁻¹ based on a consistent RV distribution and metallicity calibration.

abstract: We assigned radial velocity (RV) memberships to stars observed spectroscopically, and derived the cluster age and distance from theoretical isochrone fitting to the cluster colour-magnitude diagram.
title: A likely runaway star cluster in the outer disc of the Large Magellanic Cloud

catalog_assessment.evidence: Photometric and spectroscopic study of a specific star cluster, including radial velocity memberships, metallicity, and structural parameters.

papers.title: Resolved Kinematics of Runaway and Field OB Stars in the Small Magellanic Cloud
papers.author_names: M. S. Oey J. Dorigo Jones N. Castro P. Zivick G. Besla H. C. Januszewski M. Moe N. Kallivayalil D. J. Lennon

abstract.text: We use GAIA DR2 proper motions of the RIOTS4 field OB stars in the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) to study the kinematics of runaway stars. The data reveal that the SMC Wing has a systemic peculiar motion relative to the SMC Bar of (v_RA, v_Dec) = (62 +/-7, -18+/-5) km/s and relative radial velocity +4.5 +/- 5.0 km/s. This unambiguously demonstrates that these two regions are kinematically distinct: the Wing is moving away from the Bar, and towards the Large Magellanic Cloud with a 3-D velocity of 64 +/- 10 km/s. This is consistent with models for a recent, direct collision between the Clouds. We present transverse velocity distributions for our field OB stars, confirming that unbound runaways comprise on the order of half our sample, possibly more. Using eclipsing binaries and double-lined spectroscopic binaries as tracers of dynamically ejected runaways, and high-mass X-ray binaries (HMXBs) as tracers of runaways accelerated by supernova kicks, we find significant contributions from both populations. The data suggest that HMXBs have lower velocity dispersion relative to dynamically ejected binaries, consistent with the former corresponding to less energetic supernova kicks that failed to unbind the components. Evidence suggests that our fast runaways are dominated by dynamical, rather than supernova, ejections.

source_fields.sections_text: SMC field OB kinematics: The kinematics of the SMC Wing reveal transverse motion along the Magellanic Bridge, supporting a recent direct collision between the SMC and LMC, while field OB star proper motions identify runaway systems through dynamical ejection mechanisms, distinguishing them from the bulk stellar motion. Introduction: Runaway OB Stars: This paper makes a novel contribution by systematically analyzing the statistical properties of field OB star runaways to distinguish between dynamical ejection and supernova kick mechanisms, addressing the unresolved question of which process dominates the formation of runaway massive stars. RIOTS4 Proper Motions from GAIA: This section presents the proper motions of 315 RIOTS4 OB stars in the SMC wing derived from Gaia DR2, corrected for systemic motion and geometric perspective, revealing a distinct peculiar motion consistent with previous kinematic studies.

abstract: We use GAIA DR2 proper motions of the RIOTS4 field OB stars in the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) to study the kinematics of runaway stars.
We present transverse velocity distributions for our field OB stars, confirming that unbound runaways comprise on the order of half our sample, possibly more.
title: Resolved Kinematics of Runaway and Field OB Stars in the Small Magellanic Cloud

catalog_assessment.evidence: Analyzes GAIA DR2 proper motions of the RIOTS4 field OB stars sample to study kinematics of runaway stars, presenting transverse velocity distributions.

papers.title: Origin of a massive hyper-runaway subgiant star LAMOST-HVS1 -- implication from Gaia and follow-up spectroscopy
papers.author_names: Kohei Hattori Monica Valluri Norberto Castro Ian U. Roederer Guillaume Mahler Gourav Khullar

abstract.text: We report that LAMOST-HVS1 is a massive hyper-runaway subgiant star with mass of 8.3 Msun and super-Solar metallicity, ejected from the inner stellar disk of the Milky Way $\\\\sim$ 33 Myr ago with the intrinsic ejection velocity of $568^{+19}_{-17}$ km/s (corrected for the streaming motion of the disk), based on the proper motion data from Gaia Data Release 2 (DR2) and high-resolution spectroscopy. The extremely large ejection velocity indicates that this star was not ejected by the supernova explosion of the binary companion. Rather, it was probably ejected by a 3- or 4-body dynamical interaction with more massive objects in a high-density environment. Such a high-density environment may be attained at the core region of a young massive cluster with mass of $\\\\gtrsim 10^4$ Msun. The ejection agent that took part in the ejection of LAMOST-HVS1 may well be an intermediate mass black hole ($\\\\gtrsim$ 100 Msun), a very massive star ($\\\\gtrsim$ 100 Msun), or multiple ordinary massive stars ($\\\\gtrsim$ 30 Msun). Based on the flight time and the ejection location of LAMOST-HVS1, we argue that its ejection agent or its natal star cluster is currently located near the Norma spiral arm. The natal star cluster of LAMOST-HVS1 may be an undiscovered young massive cluster near the Norma spiral arm.

triage.label: rule-weak-llm-confirmed
triage.reason: Weak title match: runaway-related mechanisms LLM confirmed: Explicitly about a massive hyper-runaway star (LAMOST-HVS1), its origin, ejection velocity, and ejection mechanisms (dynamical interaction). Directly matches all core topics.

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.sections_text: Introduction: This paper introduces a novel method to distinguish between binary ejection (BEM) and dynamical ejection (DEM) mechanisms of massive runaway stars by leveraging high-precision Gaia astrometry to measure their ejection velocities and natal environments, thereby solving the problem of identifying the origin of runaway stars' large peculiar velocities. Ejection frequency: The ejection rate of massive hyper-runaway stars with intrinsic velocities ≥600 km s⁻¹ from the Milky Way is estimated to be at least one per 30 Myr, based on the survival time of 8-M⊙ stars and the observed presence of LAMOST-HVS1. Spectroscopic data: Spectroscopic data from LAMOST-HVS1 reveal a massive, hot subgiant star with extreme kinematics, high metallicity, and properties consistent with a hyper-runaway origin. Discussion and conclusion: LAMOST-HVS1 is an 8.3-$M_{\\odot}$ B-type subgiant star with super-solar metallicity, ejected from the inner Milky Way disk $\\sim 33$ Myr ago with an intrinsic ejection velocity of $568^{+19}_{-17}$ km s$^{-1}$, indicating it was likely ejected by a few-body dynamical interaction in a massive young stellar cluster, and suggesting the existence of an undiscovered massive young stellar cluster in the inner disk near the Norma spiral arm. Astrometric Data from Gaia-DR2: Gaia-DR2's precise proper motion measurements rule out a Galactic Center ejection origin for LAMOST-HVS1, supporting instead a disk-born hyper-runaway scenario where the star was ejected and now lies far from the Galactic plane. Ejection mechanism of LAMOST-HVS1: The binary ejection mechanism (BEM) is disfavored for LAMOST-HVS1 due to its high intrinsic ejection velocity, while the dynamical ejection mechanism (DEM)—particularly via a Hills mechanism involving an intermediate-mass black hole—can plausibly explain its hyper-runaway nature, though no observed ultra-luminous X-ray sources support the presence of such a black hole in the required region. Analysis of the orbit of LAMOST-HVS1: This section establishes the galactic coordinate system, solar position, and potential model used to analyze the orbit of LAMOST-HVS1, emphasizing that the orbital results are robust against variations in dark matter halo parameters and primarily constrained by distance uncertainties. Appendix A A note on the quality of the astrometric solution: Despite poor performance in some statistical astrometric indicators due to its negative parallax, the astrometric solution for LAMOST-HVS1 is considered reliable based on the renormalized unit weight error (RUWE) being close to 1.0, justifying the use of Gaia proper motion in the analysis. Result: Properties of the reconstructed orbit of LAMOST-HVS1: The orbital analysis of LAMOST-HVS1 reveals it was ejected from the inner disk (~3 kpc) ~33 Myr ago with an initial speed of ~568 km s⁻¹ and is currently located at ~13 kpc from the Sun, with a heliocentric distance distribution strongly peaked at smaller values (d < 17 kpc) compared to the spectroscopic estimate, improving the distance constraint from a broad distribution (up to 35 kpc) to a more precise and physically consistent value.

abstract: LAMOST-HVS1 is a massive hyper-runaway subgiant star with mass of 8.3 Msun and super-Solar metallicity, ejected from the inner stellar disk of the Milky Way $\\\\sim$ 33 Myr ago with the intrinsic ejection velocity
of $568^{+19}_{-17}$ km/s (corrected for the streaming motion of the disk), based on the proper motion data from Gaia Data Release 2 (DR2) and high-resolution spectroscopy.
The extremely large ejection velocity indicates that this star was not ejected by the supernova explosion of the binary companion.
Rather, it was probably ejected by a 3- or 4-body dynamical interaction with more massive objects in a high-density environment.
Such a high-density environment may be attained at the core region of a young massive cluster with mass of $\\\\gtrsim 10^4$ Msun.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Detailed analysis of the specific hyper-runaway star LAMOST-HVS1 using Gaia DR2 proper motions and high-resolution spectroscopy.
